parentQuestion: How does restrictive lung disease affect FEV1 and FVC?

narrative.setting: Mr. Edgar's clinic, reviewing PFT results for a patient with pulmonary fibrosis
narrative.tension: The spirometry shows FEV1 1.5L (45% predicted) and FVC 1.8L (40% predicted). Both are very low, but the ratio is 83%. What kind of disease pattern is this?

scaffolds.narrative: Ms. Frizzle draws lungs on the board. 'In restrictive disease, the lungs are like a SMALL BALLOON instead of a big one. You can't blow it up fully! Total capacity is REDUCED.'
scaffolds.q: What does 'restrictive' mean in restrictive lung disease?
options: Lungs cannot expand fully (reduced lung volumes)
scaffolds.explain: RESTRICTIVE = LUNGS CANNOT EXPAND FULLY. Total lung capacity and vital capacity are REDUCED. Think of the lungs as a stiff, small balloon that won't inflate properly. Causes: pulmonary fibrosis (stiff lung tissue), neuromuscular disease (weak breathing muscles), chest wall deformity (rigid chest cage).

wrongExplains.0: Narrowed airways = OBSTRUCTIVE disease (asthma, COPD). Restrictive disease has normal airway diameter - the problem is reduced lung expansion, not airflow.
wrongExplains.2: Air exiting too fast would be abnormally HIGH FEV1 - not a typical problem. Restrictive disease has SMALL lungs but airways flow normally.
wrongExplains.3: Breathing in restrictive disease is HARD WORK - it takes effort to expand stiff lungs or overcome chest wall problems. Breathing is definitely not easy!

scaffolds.narrative: 'Watch what happens during spirometry!' Ms. Frizzle shows the test. 'Maximum breath in - not much air enters (lungs are small/stiff). Blow out forcefully - not much air exits. BOTH FEV1 AND FVC are LOW!'
scaffolds.q: In restrictive lung disease, what happens to FVC?
scaffolds.explain: FVC DECREASES in restrictive disease! The lungs can't hold as much air (reduced total capacity), so less air is available to exhale. Example: normal FVC 5L, restrictive FVC 2.5L. The lungs are smaller or stiffer, limiting total volume.

wrongExplains.0: Small/stiff lungs can't hold MORE air! Restriction means REDUCED volumes. FVC goes down, not up.
wrongExplains.1: If FVC stayed normal with low FEV1, that would indicate OBSTRUCTION (low ratio). In restriction, FVC is also reduced.
wrongExplains.3: FVC is measurable even in severe restriction - it's just lower than normal. Complete unmeasurability would mean no breathing at all.

scaffolds.narrative: 'Here's the key!' Mr. Edgar emphasizes. 'In restriction, BOTH FEV1 and FVC drop together PROPORTIONALLY. The ratio stays normal because airways aren't obstructed - the lungs are just smaller!'
scaffolds.q: In restrictive disease, how do FEV1 and FVC change?
options: FEV1 decreases but FVC stays normal
FEV1 stays normal but FVC decreases
scaffolds.explain: Both FEV1 and FVC DECREASE PROPORTIONALLY in restrictive disease. If total lung volume is 50% of normal, both FEV1 and FVC will be about 50% of normal. The airways flow normally - there's just LESS lung to empty. This keeps the FEV1/FVC ratio normal (≥0.70).

wrongExplains.1: FEV1 low with normal FVC = OBSTRUCTIVE pattern (low ratio). In restriction, BOTH values decrease together.
wrongExplains.2: This combination doesn't match any typical pattern. Restriction affects total volume (FVC), which necessarily affects the first second (FEV1) too.
wrongExplains.3: Small stiff lungs can't have INCREASED volumes! Restriction = REDUCED lung capacity. Both measurements go down.

scaffolds.narrative: The bus flies into fibrotic lungs. 'Look at all this SCAR TISSUE!' Wanda points. 'The normal elastic alveoli are replaced by thick, stiff collagen. The lungs can't STRETCH - they're like old dried-out rubber bands!'
scaffolds.q: What causes pulmonary fibrosis (a restrictive disease)?
options: Scarring and thickening of lung tissue reducing elasticity
scaffolds.explain: Pulmonary fibrosis = SCARRING and THICKENING of lung tissue with collagen deposition, reducing elasticity. Causes: idiopathic (unknown), autoimmune diseases, chronic inflammation, drugs, radiation, asbestos. The stiff scarred lungs can't expand fully, creating restrictive pattern on PFTs.

wrongExplains.0: Bronchospasm = OBSTRUCTIVE disease (asthma). Fibrosis is scarring of the lung TISSUE itself, not airway muscle contraction.
wrongExplains.2: Fluid in airways causes crackles and can worsen gas exchange, but the restrictive pattern of fibrosis comes from permanent scarring, not fluid.
wrongExplains.3: Overinflation (hyperinflation) occurs in COPD/emphysema - actually increases total lung capacity initially. Fibrosis RESTRICTS expansion.

scaffolds.narrative: 'Other causes of restriction!' Ms. Frizzle lists them: 'Neuromuscular disease - weak breathing muscles can't expand the chest. Kyphoscoliosis - deformed spine restricts chest wall. Obesity - belly weight limits diaphragm movement!'
scaffolds.q: Which of these causes restrictive lung disease?
options: Severe kyphoscoliosis (spinal deformity limiting chest expansion)
scaffolds.explain: KYPHOSCOLIOSIS (severe spinal curvature) causes RESTRICTIVE disease by limiting chest wall expansion. The rigid, deformed chest cage can't expand fully during inspiration. Other restrictive causes: neuromuscular disease (ALS, muscular dystrophy), pleural disease, obesity, pregnancy.

wrongExplains.0: Asthma = OBSTRUCTIVE disease (narrow airways, low FEV1/FVC ratio). It doesn't restrict total lung capacity.
wrongExplains.2: Chronic bronchitis = OBSTRUCTIVE disease (part of COPD). Inflamed airways with mucus = obstruction, not restriction.
wrongExplains.3: Emphysema = OBSTRUCTIVE disease. Destroyed alveoli lose elastic recoil, airways collapse during exhalation. Low ratio.

scaffolds.narrative: 'During the test, watch the patient work!' Ms. Frizzle points. 'They're using accessory muscles, breathing fast and shallow - it's HARD to expand stiff fibrotic lungs. But when air DOES move, it flows normally through the airways!'
scaffolds.q: Why is the FEV1/FVC ratio normal in restrictive disease?
options: Airways are normal - they're not the problem; lung volumes are reduced
The patient isn't trying hard enough
Restrictive disease always has abnormal ratio
scaffolds.explain: The ratio stays NORMAL because AIRWAYS ARE NORMAL in restrictive disease! The problem is lung SIZE/COMPLIANCE, not airflow. Air flows normally through the airways - there's just less lung to empty. Both FEV1 and FVC decrease equally, maintaining ratio ≥0.70.

wrongExplains.0: If airways were obstructed, you'd have OBSTRUCTIVE disease with low ratio. Restrictive disease has open airways - the lungs are just small/stiff.
wrongExplains.2: Assuming poor effort is dangerous! If spirometry quality criteria are met, low volumes with normal ratio indicate true restrictive physiology.
wrongExplains.3: Restrictive disease characteristically has NORMAL or HIGH ratio! That's how you distinguish it from obstruction. Low ratio = obstruction.

scaffolds.narrative: Mr. Edgar reviews the CT scan. 'See this honeycomb pattern and reticular opacities? Classic pulmonary fibrosis. With low FEV1, low FVC, but normal ratio on PFTs, everything fits restrictive disease.'
scaffolds.q: A patient has FEV1 1.5L (45%), FVC 1.8L (40%), ratio 83%. What pattern is this?
scaffolds.explain: RESTRICTIVE pattern: Both FEV1 and FVC are LOW (45% and 40% of predicted), but ratio is NORMAL (83%, which is >70%). Both volumes reduced proportionally. Think fibrosis, neuromuscular disease, or chest wall restriction. Need full PFTs (TLC) to confirm.

wrongExplains.0: The ratio is 83% - that's ABOVE 70% cutoff, so not obstructive! Low ratio would be <70%. This is restrictive (normal ratio, low volumes).
wrongExplains.2: Both values are well below predicted (45%, 40%) - definitely NOT normal! Normal would be >80% predicted for both.
wrongExplains.3: Mixed would show low volumes AND low ratio (<70%). This has normal ratio (83%), indicating pure restriction without obstruction component.

scaffolds.narrative: 'To fully confirm restriction,' Mr. Edgar explains, 'we need FULL pulmonary function tests measuring TLC (total lung capacity). Spirometry suggests restriction, but TLC definitively proves it by showing reduced total volume.'
scaffolds.q: What additional test confirms restrictive disease?
options: Total lung capacity (TLC) measurement showing reduced volumes
scaffolds.explain: TOTAL LUNG CAPACITY (TLC) measurement confirms restriction. Spirometry (FEV1/FVC) suggests restriction, but TLC definitively proves it. Measured by body plethysmography or gas dilution. TLC <80% predicted confirms restriction. Obstructive disease can sometimes appear restrictive on spirometry alone.

wrongExplains.0: Chest X-ray shows structural abnormalities (fibrosis, effusion) but doesn't measure lung VOLUMES. You need PFTs with TLC measurement for physiologic confirmation.
wrongExplains.2: Bronchodilator testing distinguishes reversible from fixed obstruction (asthma vs. COPD), not obstruction from restriction.
wrongExplains.3: Restriction is very confirmable! Full PFTs with TLC measurement definitively diagnose and quantify restrictive disease.
